TextBlock.Text: SOVIET WARSHIPS COLLIDE WITH U.S. NAVY IN BLACK SEA; U.S. CLAIMS INNOCENT TRANSIT

StackPanel.TextBlock:
NORFOLK, VIRGINIA — Tensions spiked yesterday as two Soviet warships intentionally collided with U.S. Navy vessels operating in the Black Sea. The incident, which took place near the Crimean Peninsula, involved the guided missile cruiser USS Yorktown (CG-48) and destroyer USS Caron (DD-970), which were conducting what the Navy calls “innocent passage” through international waters.
According to U.S. officials, the Soviet Krivak-class frigate Bezzavetnyy and a Mirka-class frigate aggressively maneuvered across the bows of both American ships, making contact at low speed. No injuries were reported, though Caron sustained superficial damage to her port side.
Defense Secretary Frank Carlucci condemned the Soviet actions, calling them “reckless and escalatory.” The Kremlin has defended the ramming as a defensive measure, accusing the United States of “provocative surveillance” near sensitive Soviet facilities. (see COLLISION on pg. 2)

TextBlock.Text: Soviet frigate Bezzavetnyy collides with U.S. cruiser Yorktown. USS Caron was also deliberately struck by Soviet warships asserting territorial seas. The deliberate ramming occurred as part of an encounter between U.S. Navy vessels conducting freedom of navigation exercises and Soviet forces asserting expanding territorial sea claims. — US Navy photo

StackPanel.TextBlock:
ROTA, SPAIN — In a decisive show of force, the U.S. Navy has redirected a Surface Action Group (SAG) from its Mediterranean operations to the North Sea, following heightened Soviet naval activity in the North Atlantic and Barents Sea. The task force, which departed the U.S. Naval Station in Rota early Saturday morning, is expected to reinforce NATO’s northern maritime defenses within the week.
This move comes as Soviet missile tests in the Barents Sea and increased submarine incursions into the North Atlantic have put NATO forces on high alert. Intelligence reports indicate that several Soviet cruisers and destroyers have deployed north of the GIUK (Greenland-Iceland-UK) Gap, a maritime chokepoint that controls access between the Atlantic and Arctic waters. (see NAVY on pg.4)

MOSCOW — In a move that has further strained East-West relations, the Soviet Union has announced a series of planned missile tests in the Barents Sea, fuelling concerns within NATO about Moscow’s military intentions. The tests, scheduled to take place within the next two weeks, will involve both submarine-launched and ground-based ballistic missiles.
The Kremlin has framed the missile launches as part of a “routine readiness exercise” meant to validate the Soviet Union’s defensive capabilities. However, Western analysts fear the timing of the tests (amid several naval confrontations) signals a show of force rather than standard drills. (see MISSILES on pg.7)
